questions.question: Explain the structure of a TCP segment header. List all fields in order with their sizes.
keyPoints: Source Port (16 bits) - identifies sending application
Destination Port (16 bits) - identifies receiving application
Sequence Number (32 bits) - byte position in data stream
Acknowledgment Number (32 bits) - next expected byte
Data Offset (4 bits) - header length in 32-bit words
Reserved (6 bits) - for future use
Control Flags (6 bits) - URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN
Window Size (16 bits) - flow control
Checksum (16 bits) - error detection
Urgent Pointer (16 bits) - urgent data pointer
Options (0-40 bytes) - optional fields
Data (variable) - payload

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Sequence Number and Acknowledgment Number in TCP? How are they used to ensure reliable data transfer?
keyPoints: Sequence Number: positions first data byte in segment within entire stream
Acknowledgment Number: confirms received bytes, indicates next expected byte
Enables detection of missing segments (gaps in sequence)
Sender retransmits if ACK not received within timeout
Cumulative ACK acknowledges all bytes up to specified number
Allows ordered delivery of data to application

questions.question: Describe the TCP three-way handshake process. What flags are used and what information is exchanged?
keyPoints: Step 1: Client sends SYN segment (SYN=1, seq=x)
Step 2: Server sends SYN+ACK (SYN=1, ACK=1, seq=y, ack=x+1)
Step 3: Client sends ACK (ACK=1, seq=x+1, ack=y+1)
Purpose: Synchronize sequence numbers, establish connection
After 3-way handshake, connection is ESTABLISHED
Sequence numbers are exchanged for reliable data transfer

questions.question: How is TCP checksum calculated? Explain the role of the pseudo-header in this calculation.
keyPoints: TCP checksum is 16-bit field for error detection
Pseudo-header is constructed with: Source IP (32-bit), Dest IP (32-bit), Protocol (8-bit=6), TCP Length (16-bit)
Pseudo-header + TCP header + data are summed as 16-bit words
One's complement of sum is stored as checksum
Receiver recalculates: if result = 0xFFFF, segment is valid
Pseudo-header ensures IP addresses aren't corrupted

questions.question: Explain the significance of the Window Size field in TCP. How does it implement flow control?
keyPoints: Window Size is 16-bit field indicating receive buffer capacity
Receiver advertises available buffer space in this field
Sender can transmit up to 'window' bytes without receiving ACK
Receiver sends updated Window Size in ACK segments
If Window = 0, sender must stop and wait (zero window)
Window Scaling option extends this to 16 bits for high-speed networks

questions.question: What are TCP options? List the commonly used options and their purposes.
keyPoints: Options extend TCP header beyond minimum 20 bytes
Format: Kind (1 byte), Length (1 byte), Value (variable)
Maximum 40 bytes of options (since max header = 60 bytes)
Common options: MSS (Kind 2) - max segment size
Window Scaling (Kind 3) - expand window beyond 65535
Timestamp (Kind 8) - RTT estimation and PAWS
SACK (Kind 4,5) - selective acknowledgment of out-of-order data

questions.question: Explain the difference between TCP and UDP segment structures. Why is UDP called a connectionless protocol?
keyPoints: UDP header is 8 bytes fixed; TCP minimum header is 20 bytes
UDP has: Source Port, Dest Port, Length, Checksum (no seq/ack/window)
UDP is connectionless: no handshake, no state, no reliability
UDP doesn't guarantee delivery, order, or error correction
TCP provides: connection-oriented, reliable, ordered, flow-controlled
UDP is simpler, faster, used for real-time applications (DNS, VoIP)

questions.question: A client sends a TCP segment with Sequence Number = 1000, Acknowledgment Number = 5000, and 800 bytes of data. The receiver's advertised window size is 2000 bytes. Answer the following: (a) What do these values mean? (b) What will be the sequence number of the next segment from client? (c) If the receiver responds with ACK = 1800, what does this indicate? (d) What happens if the receiver's window becomes 0?
keyPoints: (a) Seq=1000: first data byte at position 1000; Ack=5000: expect byte 5000 next (all up to 4999 received); Window=2000: receiver buffer can accept 2000 bytes
(c) ACK=1800 confirms all bytes up to 1799 received, next expected is 1800
(d) Zero window: sender stops transmitting, waits for Window Update (probe), uses persist timer to periodically check if window reopened

questions.question: Explain the TCP connection termination process (four-way handshake). Why is it necessary to have four segments instead of two?
keyPoints: Step 1: Client sends FIN (seq=x) - closes client-to-server direction
Step 2: Server sends ACK (ack=x+1) - acknowledges client's FIN
Step 3: Server sends FIN (seq=y) - closes server-to-client direction
Step 4: Client sends ACK (ack=y+1) - acknowledges server's FIN
Why 4-way: Each direction must be closed independently
Full duplex: data can flow both ways until explicitly closed
Server may have more data to send after receiving client's FIN (half-close)
After final ACK, both sides wait 2MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) before releasing resources

questions.question: Consider a scenario where a TCP segment arrives at the receiver with the following header fields: Data Offset = 7, Flags = 000110. (a) What is the header length? (b) What does the flag value indicate? (c) If the sequence number is 5000 and segment contains 100 bytes of data, what is the acknowledgment number expected in response? (d) What will be the next sequence number from sender?
keyPoints: (a) Header length = 7 × 4 = 28 bytes (7 × 32-bit words)
(b) Flags = 000110 means bits 2 and 3 are set: PSH (push) and ACK (acknowledge). This is a data segment with push flag.
(c) ACK response should have Ack = 5000 + 100 = 5100 (next expected byte)
(d) Next sequence number = 5000 + 100 = 5100 (next byte position in stream)
